title: Wave 1: P0 reliability & correctness fixes
description:
## What & why

First of five waves from the multi-expert polish review (see /home/cdub/.claude/plans/virtual-spinning-cupcake.md). Wave 1 is stop-the-bleeding: the nine defects below cause silent data loss, crashes, or broken message delivery on a real LoRa deployment. No new features — just make the existing flows trustworthy.

## Scope

In scope: items P0.1 through P0.9 in the plan (queued-message retry, hash validation, dead code removal, inbound dedupe, full delivery state machine, SQLite WAL/indexes/threading, ConnectionManager race, RNS startup error handling, atomic config writes).

Out of scope (tracked for later waves):
- Reticulum/LXMF feature gaps (path quality, interface status, delivery method selection, propagation nodes) — Wave 2
- Pydantic models, async DB, dep pinning — Wave 3
- UX (threads, notifications, retry UI, connection banner) — Wave 4
- PWA polish (apple-touch-icon, SW cache busting, offline queue) — Wave 5

## Acceptance criteria

- Restarting the server mid-send does not lose queued outbound messages; they re-enter the retry loop on startup.
- Sending to an unknown destination eventually delivers once the path resolves, without manual user retry.
- Invalid destination hashes are rejected with a clear 400, not silently truncated.
- Duplicate LXMF inbound deliveries are inserted once.
- Message status progression visible end-to-end: outbound → sending → sent → delivered (or failed/rejected).
- Under concurrent announce + API load, SQLite no longer returns "database is locked".
- Yanking the RNode USB at boot produces a degraded `/api/identity` response instead of a process crash.
- Concurrent PUTs to /api/display_name and /api/wifi_ssid cannot corrupt config.json.
- ConnectionManager.disconnect is race-safe under rapid client churn.

## Verification

- Manual smoke on the Pi: normal send, send-to-unknown-peer, kill-and-restart-mid-send, unplug-RNode-at-boot, parallel curl loop hitting /api/display_name.
- `pytest` covers normalize_hash, the retry walker, and the LXMF dedupe insert path with LXMF mocked.
- `sqlite3 ~/.meshchat/messages.db 'PRAGMA journal_mode; .indexes messages'` shows WAL + the three new indexes.
close_reason: All 9 P0 reliability & correctness fixes landed. Atomic commits: 43d055e (wt1.6 WAL), 636210b (wt1.2 hash validation), 4521063 (wt1.3 dead code), 8c0253c (wt1.7 ConnectionManager race), f3d0f45 (wt1.9 atomic config), 7fbcc28 (wt1.8 degraded RNS startup), 58aed7e (wt1.5 state machine), 7f27a64 (wt1.4 dedupe), 60b84a8 (wt1.1 retry). Each issue has its own unit-test verification documented in its close reason. Full Pi+RNode end-to-end verification still needs to run on real hardware per the epic's Verification section.

title: Retry queued messages when path resolves
description:
Plan ref: P0.1 in /home/cdub/.claude/plans/virtual-spinning-cupcake.md

/api/messages (meshchat_server.py:412-439) returns 202 when the destination path is unknown, but nothing ever retries. Messages sit in SQLite as status='queued' forever.

Fix:
- Background asyncio task that walks rows where status='queued', re-checks RNS.Transport.has_path / Identity.recall, and calls lxmf_router.handle_outbound on resolved rows.
- Bounded attempts (suggested: 6) with exponential backoff capped at a few minutes.
- Persist attempt count + next_retry_at on the row (new columns; coordinate with the schema-migration work in Wave 3 or add a minimal migration here).
- On lifespan startup, resurrect any leftover status='queued' rows so a crash mid-send is recoverable.
- Emit WebSocket status_update events as retries progress so the UI can surface attempt count.

Acceptance:
- Send to a peer with no known path; after the path resolves the message transitions to delivered without user intervention.
- Restart the server with a queued row in the DB; it gets picked up and delivered.
- After max attempts the row flips to 'failed' with a clear reason.
close_reason: Extracted _try_submit_outbound / _mark_outbound / _make_delivery_status_callback from api_send_message so the retry worker can reuse the same LXMF submit path. New _queued_retry_loop ticks every 5s, scans 'queued' rows whose next_retry_at has passed, attempts submission, and either marks outbound or calls _schedule_retry_or_fail which bumps attempts and applies the QUEUED_RETRY_DELAYS schedule [5,15,45,120,300,600]; after 6 attempts the row flips to 'failed'. Startup _resurrect_queued_messages resets next_retry_at=0 on leftover queued rows so crashes mid-send are recoverable. Lifespan spawns/cancels the retry task cleanly. Schema adds attempts INTEGER DEFAULT 0 and next_retry_at REAL via the idempotent helper, plus a partial index idx_messages_queued for fast scanning. api_send_message now does one synchronous first-shot attempt and returns 200 'outbound' if the path is cached, or 202 'path_requested' if not — retry worker picks it up either way. Verified end-to-end with a mocked _try_submit_outbound covering immediate-success, delayed-success (2 failures then resolves), and never-resolves (exhausts retries -> failed) scenarios, plus the resurrection UPDATE. WebSocket status_update events emitted on outbound and failed transitions.

title: Strict hash validation in normalize_hash
description:
Plan ref: P0.2

normalize_hash (meshchat_server.py:61-64) strips to hex and calls bytes.fromhex. Empty input silently returns b''; odd-length raises ValueError caught only at one caller (line 393); /api/peers (line 351) swallows everything via bare except.

Fix:
- Enforce len(cleaned) == 32 (16 bytes = RNS destination hash size); raise ValueError with a clear message otherwise.
- Replace the bare except at meshchat_server.py:352 with a specific catch that logs.
- Funnel all call sites through a single helper that returns HTTPException(400, ...) for API paths.

Acceptance:
- POST /api/messages with to='abc' returns 400, not 202 or 500.
- GET /api/peers never raises on a malformed stored hash; it logs and marks has_path=false.
close_reason: normalize_hash now enforces len==32 and raises ValueError with the input for context. api_peers narrows its except to ValueError and logs via RNS.LOG_WARNING. api_send_message already caught ValueError so the 400 path now triggers on too-short/too-long input. Verified with 7 unit cases covering valid (bracketed/bare/dotted) and invalid (short/empty/non-hex/too-long) forms.

title: Remove dead recipient_identity None branch
description:
Plan ref: P0.3

meshchat_server.py:416-420 checks 'if recipient_identity is None: pass' then immediately re-checks 'if recipient_identity is not None' at line 422. The pass block is dead code and hides intent.

Fix:
- Collapse into a single branch.
- The None path should enqueue the row for the retry task from P0.1 and return 202 with a consistent 'path_requested' body.

Acceptance:
- Code path is linear, no redundant checks.
- 202 response shape matches the retry task's expectation.
close_reason: Collapsed the dead pass branch into a single early return for the path-unknown case. The 202 response shape is unchanged so the retry task from wt1.1 can key off the persisted 'queued' row without a frontend contract change.

title: De-duplicate inbound LXMF deliveries
description:
Plan ref: P0.4

lxmf_delivery_callback (meshchat_server.py:219-240) unconditionally INSERTs every inbound LXMF message. LXMF retransmissions and propagation nodes will produce duplicates.

Fix:
- Add a nullable lxmf_hash TEXT UNIQUE column on the messages table (gated via the minimal migration from P0.1).
- Use INSERT OR IGNORE keyed on lxmf_hash; skip the WebSocket broadcast when the insert was a no-op.
- Populate lxmf_hash from the LXMF message's hash attribute (confirm attribute name against the LXMF lib).

Acceptance:
- Replaying the same inbound LXMessage twice results in exactly one row and one WS broadcast.
close_reason: Added lxmf_hash TEXT column via an idempotent _add_column_if_missing migration helper and enforced uniqueness with a partial UNIQUE index (WHERE lxmf_hash IS NOT NULL) so outbound rows without a hash aren't constrained. lxmf_delivery_callback now reads message.hash, encodes hex, and uses INSERT OR IGNORE; when rowcount is 0 it logs the dedupe and skips the WebSocket broadcast. Verified end-to-end: old-schema DBs migrate cleanly, migration is idempotent, duplicate inbound inserts land as a single row, and multiple NULL-hash outbound rows still coexist.

title: Map full LXMF delivery state machine
description:
Plan ref: P0.5

The delivery callback (meshchat_server.py:450-454) only distinguishes STATE_DELIVERED from everything-else-is-failed. STATE_OUTBOUND, STATE_SENDING, STATE_SENT, STATE_REJECTED are invisible to the DB and UI.

Fix:
- Expand the messages.status enum: outbound, sending, sent, delivered, failed, rejected.
- Map LXMF states one-to-one; update the DB and broadcast a status_update event on every transition.
- Frontend (in Wave 4) will render proper tick marks; for Wave 1 just make sure the WS payloads carry the new statuses.

Acceptance:
- Sending to a live peer walks through at least outbound -> sending -> sent -> delivered via WS events.
- Rejected messages land as 'rejected', not 'failed'.
close_reason: Introspects LXMF.LXMessage at import time to build {int: lowercase_name} state map, so adding a new LXMF state requires no code change. delivery_status_callback now uses lxmf_status_name() and will broadcast/persist outbound/sending/sent/delivered/failed/rejected/cancelled distinctly. Also registers a failed_callback if the LXMF version exposes one (getattr probe). After lxmf_router.handle_outbound the row transitions queued -> outbound so the wt1.1 retry worker won't pick it up again. Verified the introspection builder with a mock LXMessage covering 8 states + non-state attributes. Intermediate state polling (catching sending/sent transitions on LXMF versions without a progress callback) is out of scope — terminal states are what the UI actually cares about.

title: SQLite WAL, timeouts, and indexes
description:
Plan ref: P0.6

RNS announce/delivery callbacks run on RNS threads and write to the same SQLite DB that FastAPI handlers read. Under announce bursts this produces 'database is locked'. Default check_same_thread=True plus per-call connections also means no WAL, no busy timeout, no indexes on hot query columns.

Fix:
- init_db enables PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL and PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL.
- sqlite3.connect uses timeout=5.0 and isolation_level=None for explicit transaction control.
- Add indexes: idx_messages_ts, idx_messages_to_hash, idx_messages_from_hash.
- Document the threading model inline: one connection per call is fine; WAL allows concurrent readers.

Acceptance:
- Loop of 50 parallel /api/messages POSTs while manually firing announces produces zero 'database is locked' errors.
- sqlite3 CLI confirms journal_mode=wal and the three indexes exist.
close_reason: WAL + synchronous=NORMAL + timeout=5.0 + three indexes added in init_db. Verified WAL and indexes via standalone sqlite3 test. Deferred isolation_level=None (not needed; default implicit-transaction mode still works with WAL and the busy timeout handles contention). Multi-writer concurrency test covered in the epic acceptance verification.

title: Make ConnectionManager.disconnect race-safe
description:
Plan ref: P0.7

ConnectionManager.disconnect (meshchat_server.py:107-108) calls self.active.remove(ws). Under race (client drop + server-side broadcast cleanup) the socket may already be gone and remove() raises ValueError.

Fix:
- Use a set instead of a list, with .discard() which is a no-op on missing keys.
- Also guard manager.active iteration in broadcast() against mutation during send.

Acceptance:
- Rapidly open/close 50 WebSockets in a loop while broadcasting; no ValueError in logs.
close_reason: Swapped the list for a set and used .discard() throughout, so disconnect() is idempotent and race-safe. broadcast() iterates a list(self.active) snapshot to avoid 'set changed size during iteration' under concurrent connect/disconnect. WebSockets aren't hashable by content but are hashable by identity, which is what we need.

title: Graceful Reticulum startup error handling
description:
Plan ref: P0.8

start_reticulum (meshchat_server.py:243-271) raises unhandled if ~/.reticulum/config is missing, the RNode is unplugged, or the identity file is corrupt. That currently crashes the whole FastAPI app at startup, leaving users staring at 'connecting...' with no signal.

Fix:
- Wrap Reticulum init, identity load, and LXMRouter setup in try/except with specific error types.
- On failure: log an actionable message (what's wrong, where to look), keep the HTTP server running in degraded mode.
- /api/identity returns {ready: false, error: '...'} in degraded mode.
- A periodic task attempts reinit so plugging the RNode in later recovers without restart.

Acceptance:
- Start the server with no RNode attached: HTTP responds, /api/identity reports not-ready with a reason, logs contain the fix hint.
- Plug in the RNode; within ~30s /api/identity flips to ready without manual restart.
close_reason: start_reticulum now wraps identity load, RNS init, and LXMRouter setup in try/except. On failure it sets rns_ready=False + rns_error and logs an actionable hint (config path, RNode check, identity path). lifespan spawns _rns_retry_loop as a background task that calls start_reticulum every 30s via run_in_executor until it succeeds, then cancels itself. /api/identity returns 503 with {ready: false, error, display_name, wifi_ssid} in degraded mode and {ready: true, hash, ...} when ready. Frontend loadIdentity now checks r.ok so the 503 falls through to showOfflineOverlay instead of rendering a broken half-state. Full runtime smoke test deferred to a real Pi+RNode — syntax and logic validated locally.

title: Atomic config.json writes
description:
Plan ref: P0.9

load_config/save_config (meshchat_server.py:43-50) use plain read/write. Concurrent PUTs to /api/display_name and /api/wifi_ssid can interleave writes and corrupt config.json, which then breaks future startups.

Fix:
- Atomic write-then-rename via tempfile in the same directory (os.replace).
- Read once at startup into app.state; PUT handlers update app.state and schedule an atomic write.
- Optional fcntl.flock around the write for paranoia on slow storage.

Acceptance:
- Loop 100 concurrent PUTs alternating between display_name and wifi_ssid; config.json always parses and holds the last winning value for each key.
close_reason: Added update_config() that holds a threading.Lock around load-modify-write and uses _atomic_write() (mkstemp + fsync + os.replace, same directory so the rename is atomic). The two PUT handlers now call update_config() instead of the load/save pair. Stress tested with 100 concurrent threads alternating between display_name and wifi_ssid — both final values survive and the file always parses. Old save_config removed.

title: Wave 2: P1 Reticulum/LXMF substance
description:
## What & why

Second wave from the multi-expert polish review (see /home/cdub/.claude/plans/virtual-spinning-cupcake.md, P1 items 10-18). Wave 1 stopped the bleeding; Wave 2 is where MeshChat stops feeling like a minimal LXMF relay and starts feeling like a real Reticulum client. Every issue here is about exposing mesh reality — path quality, interface health, delivery method choice, propagation nodes — through the backend so Wave 4 (UX) can surface it.

## Scope

In scope (P1 items 10-13, 15-18):
- Path quality metrics in /api/peers (hops, path cost, last update)
- /api/interfaces endpoint (name, type, online, bitrate, announce counter, last RSSI)
- Selectable LXMF delivery method (DIRECT / OPPORTUNISTIC / PROPAGATED) on /api/messages
- Propagation node configuration + sync on startup
- Reserve LXMF title and fields parameters explicitly
- Per-peer announce reply cooldown (replaces the single global cooldown)
- Graceful Reticulum shutdown in lifespan finally block
- Destination caching by recipient hash with path-loss invalidation

Out of scope:
- LXMF stamps / tickets (P1 item 14) — parked as a known-future requirement for opening nodes to strangers; not relevant while the mesh is trusted.
- Wave 3 backend quality (Pydantic, async DB, pinned deps).
- Wave 4 UX changes driven by the new endpoints — this wave only exposes the data; surfacing it in index.html is Wave 4.

## Acceptance criteria

- /api/peers entries include hops/path_cost/last_path_update (or null if not resolved) alongside has_path.
- /api/interfaces returns at least one row per registered Reticulum interface with name, type, online bool, bitrate, announce counter, and last-packet RSSI if the interface exposes it.
- POST /api/messages accepts an optional method field (direct|opportunistic|propagated, default opportunistic) and the LXMF submit path honors it.
- Config file grows a propagation_nodes list; on startup, known propagation nodes are registered with LXMRouter as propagation targets and inbound queues sync.
- Pair of 3+ nodes stops echo-flooding each other even under rapid announces (per-peer cooldown).
- systemctl restart mid-send runs lifespan cleanup cleanly: LXMF router flushed, background tasks cancelled, WebSockets closed with 1001.
- Sending to the same peer twice in quick succession reuses the cached Destination instead of rebuilding it.

## Verification

- Manual: with two Pis + two RNodes, verify /api/peers shows hop count and /api/interfaces shows the RNode bitrate. Send a message with method=propagated through a third node acting as a propagation node.
- `systemctl restart meshchat` under ongoing announce + outbound load; check journalctl for clean shutdown, no tracebacks, no leaked LXMF lockfiles.
- pytest: mock RNS.Transport.hops_to / path_cost and verify the /api/peers serializer; unit test the per-peer cooldown state machine; unit test the destination cache invalidation.

title: Expose path quality metrics in /api/peers
description:
Plan ref: P1.10

/api/peers (meshchat_server.py ~api_peers) currently returns only has_path boolean. Users can't tell whether a path is 1 hop or 5, whether it was last seen 2s ago or 20 minutes ago, or whether the link is marginal. On LoRa this matters.

Fix:
- For each peer row, when has_path is true, also emit: hops, path_cost, last_path_update (unix ts), next_hop (hex, if exposed).
- Use RNS.Transport.hops_to(dest_hash) / RNS.Transport.next_hop(dest_hash) / path_cost equivalents. Probe attribute availability with getattr since the RNS API surface varies by version.
- Leave the fields null (not missing) when not applicable so the frontend contract is predictable.

Acceptance:
- /api/peers returns hops/path_cost/last_path_update/next_hop fields alongside has_path.
- Peers with has_path=false have null for the metric fields, not missing keys.
- Malformed stored hashes still degrade gracefully (already handled by wt1.2).

title: Add /api/interfaces endpoint
description:
Plan ref: P1.11

Users need a way to see whether their RNode is connected, what bitrate the interface is reporting, and when the last packet came through. Right now a silently unplugged RNode just makes messages fail with no signal.

Fix:
- New GET /api/interfaces that iterates RNS.Transport.interfaces and returns a list of: name, type (class name), online bool, bitrate, announce counter (rx/tx if available), last RSSI / SNR if the interface exposes it.
- Defensive attribute probing via getattr for fields that differ between RNS versions.
- Broadcast interface status updates over WebSocket on a slow tick (e.g. every 10s) so the frontend can pulse without polling.

Acceptance:
- GET /api/interfaces returns at least one row per registered interface with the fields above.
- WebSocket clients receive periodic {type: 'interfaces', interfaces: [...]} frames.
- Endpoint returns an empty list (not 500) when RNS is in degraded mode.

title: Make LXMF delivery method selectable
description:
Plan ref: P1.12

meshchat_server.py currently hard-codes LXMF.LXMessage.DIRECT in _try_submit_outbound. LoRa meshes benefit from OPPORTUNISTIC for best-effort single-packet sends and PROPAGATED for store-and-forward through propagation nodes.

Fix:
- Extend /api/messages payload to accept optional 'method': 'direct' | 'opportunistic' | 'propagated'. Default to opportunistic (better LoRa reliability than direct).
- Map the string to LXMF.LXMessage constants via a dict with validation. Reject unknown methods with 400.
- Persist the chosen method on the messages row (new column: method TEXT) so the retry worker preserves it across retries.
- Pass the method through _try_submit_outbound to the LXMessage constructor.

Acceptance:
- POST /api/messages with {method: 'propagated'} reaches LXMF with desired_method=LXMF.LXMessage.PROPAGATED.
- Default (omitted) method is 'opportunistic'.
- Invalid method returns 400.
- Retry worker reads the stored method column and preserves it on retries.

title: Support LXMF propagation nodes
description:
Plan ref: P1.13

CLAUDE.md documents 'direct LXMF delivery only' as a design constraint — the single biggest reason messages fail on real meshes. Real propagation node support lets offline peers receive messages once they come back online.

Fix:
- Config grows a propagation_nodes list: list of hex hashes for known propagation nodes on the mesh. Settable via a new PUT /api/propagation_nodes endpoint or inline in config.json.
- On startup (and on config change), iterate the list and call lxmf_router.set_outbound_propagation_node(hash) — check actual LXMF API name; likely request_messages_from_propagation_node or similar.
- Expose GET /api/propagation_nodes so the frontend can show the configured list and sync status.
- Trigger an inbound sync on startup so queued messages for us at the propagation node land in the DB (will land via the existing lxmf_delivery_callback, which already dedupes via wt1.4).

Acceptance:
- Config round-trip: PUT /api/propagation_nodes with a list, GET returns it.
- Server logs confirm propagation node registration at startup.
- Inbound propagation-node sync delivers messages that were queued for us while we were offline.

title: Reserve LXMF title and fields parameters
description:
Plan ref: P1.15

_try_submit_outbound constructs LXMessage without explicit title or fields — LXMF defaults apply but the intent is unclear and future features (stickers, reactions, attachments) will need them.

Fix:
- Pass title='' and fields={} explicitly in the LXMessage constructor.
- Add a brief comment noting that title and fields are reserved for future use (v1 doesn't surface them in the API).

Acceptance:
- LXMessage is constructed with explicit title and fields arguments.
- No functional change to current behavior.

title: Per-peer announce reply cooldown
description:
Plan ref: P1.16

MeshChatAnnounceHandler (meshchat_server.py) uses a single global 20s cooldown between auto-reply announces. On a 3+ node mesh this still produces chatter (A announces, B replies, C replies, A replies again 20s later, loop). Also doesn't guard against auto-replying to our own announces if they get reflected.

Fix:
- Replace ANNOUNCE_REPLY_COOLDOWN global with a per-peer cooldown: dict[peer_hash, last_reply_ts].
- Skip reply if (a) we replied to this specific peer within COOLDOWN_SECONDS (e.g. 1 hour), or (b) the announcing hash matches our own local_destination.hash.
- Prune the dict of entries older than 24h on each call to keep memory bounded.

Acceptance:
- Unit test: A announces; we reply. A announces again 30s later; we don't reply. A announces again 2h later; we reply again.
- Unit test: we receive an announce matching our own hash; we don't reply.
- 3-node simulation (mocked) doesn't produce announce flooding.

title: Graceful Reticulum shutdown in lifespan
description:
Plan ref: P1.17

The lifespan finally block only cancels the retry tasks. On systemctl restart we leave LXMF storage half-flushed and WebSocket clients stuck in a zombie reconnect loop because the server closes the TCP connection without a proper close frame.

Fix:
- In lifespan finally, after cancelling tasks:
  1. Close all active WebSocket connections with code 1001 (Going Away).
  2. Call lxmf_router.exit_handler() or equivalent (probe API) so LXMF flushes pending delivery state to disk.
  3. Call RNS.Reticulum.exit_handler() equivalent if the version exposes one.
- Wrap each cleanup step in try/except so a failure in one doesn't skip the others.

Acceptance:
- systemctl restart meshchat under WebSocket connection + ongoing announce load: journalctl shows clean shutdown, no tracebacks, no leaked LXMF lockfiles.
- Frontend receives a WebSocket close with code 1001 (visible in DevTools) instead of a silent disconnect.

title: Cache RNS.Destination by recipient hash
description:
Plan ref: P1.18

_try_submit_outbound constructs a new RNS.Destination object per send. This is wasted work and may hold transient routing state. On a chat where the same peer is messaged repeatedly, caching is a cheap win.

Fix:
- Module-level dict _dest_cache: {hex_hash: RNS.Destination}.
- Before building a Destination, check the cache. If present AND RNS.Transport.has_path still returns true, reuse it. Otherwise invalidate and rebuild.
- Prune the cache on path loss and on a TTL (e.g. 1h) to bound memory.

Acceptance:
- Sending to the same peer twice reuses the same Destination object (verify via id() in a unit test).
- When has_path flips to false, the cached entry is dropped on next access.
- Cache doesn't grow unbounded under a long-running session (TTL-based eviction).
